risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Randomization and packaging was organized by an independent statistician at the host site, using the SPSS software Rand function." Comment: This explicitly describes a computerized randomization method (Rand function) and indicates independent organization, which supports a truly random allocation sequence. The additional description of random assignment in blocks of random assignments further confirms proper random sequence generation.

Quote: "To allow participants to be recruited at satellite centres without computer links, randomization was by sealed envelope. Numbered envelopes containing a random treatment allocation were provided to each site. On enrolment participants selected their envelope from a large batch and signed it before breaking the seal to reveal their allocation." Comment: This explicitly describes a sequentially numbered sealed-envelope process with treatment allocations concealed until the participant opened the envelope, which prevents recruiters from foreseeing the next assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This was an open-label parallel-group trial" and "we could not include placebos to allow a no-treatment condition or to blind treatment allocation". Comment: Participants and personnel were not blinded to treatment assignment, and the outcomes assessed included smoking cessation and unwanted symptoms, both of which could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of allocation (e.g. adherence, co-interventions, self-reporting, and support interactions).

Quote: "This was an open-label parallel-group trial" and "Blind follow-ups were not considered feasible, due to patients usually discussing their treatment when contacted." Also, the primary outcome was defined as "a self-report ... and recording an ECO level" and secondary measures likewise relied on "self-report" plus ECO; adverse symptoms were self-reported on weekly forms. Comment: Outcome assessment was not blinded, and several key outcomes were patient self-reported smoking status and self-reported unwanted symptoms. Because the patient is the assessor for self-report outcomes and participants were unblinded, knowledge of allocation could influence outcome measurement. The study also reports subjective symptom outcomes, so the domain is High risk under the worst-outcome rule.

Quote: "The two follow-ups for the primary outcome (session 7, 26 weeks) were completed by 659 (61.5%) participants and this rate did not differ significantly between treatment groups (χ2(2) = 3.33, P = 0.19). Of the 412 participants not completing both follow-ups, 241 (58.5%) reported smoking at one follow-up which they did complete... This gave a primary outcome completion rate of 84%. Again, this rate did not differ between treatment groups." Comment: Although only 61.5% completed both follow-ups, the paper reports that for the primary outcome the effective completion rate was 84% because many non-completers were observed smoking at one follow-up and therefore could be classified as failures. The attrition appears balanced across arms, and there is no evidence of a differential loss ≥10 percentage points or total loss ≥30% in any arm for the analyzed primary outcome. The paper also states ITT analysis was used and provides follow-up completion information, so the available data do not suggest attrition bias for this domain.
